1: בַּלֵּילוֹת (baleilot) — 'in the nights' — the plural is significant: this is not a single night but a recurring pattern of nighttime searching. The plural leilot suggests the anxious nights of longing rather than one specific occasion, giving the passage a dream-sequence or habitual quality. בִּקַּשְׁתִּיו (biqqashtihu) — 'I sought him' — the Piel intensive of baqash (to seek, to want urgently); the Piel intensifies the seeking. The phrase she'ahavah nafshi (the one my soul loves) is repeated three more times in this passage (vv2, 3, 4), creating a refrain of urgent longing.
2: אָקוּמָה נָּא (aqumah na) — 'let me arise, please' — the particle na marks the resolve as tentative, a persuasion of herself; she debates and then decides. אֲסוֹבְבָה (asobbevah) — 'I will go around/circuit' — the Polel of savav (to surround, to go around) suggests circling the city, searching all its circuits. בָּרְחֹבוֹת וּבַשְּׁוָקִים (barechobbot uvashvakim) — 'in the plazas and in the marketplaces' — the two public spaces of an ancient city: the broad open areas and the commercial streets. She will not hide her search in private but will make it public.
3: שֹׁמְרִים הַסֹּבְבִים בָּעִיר (shomrim hasovevim ba'ir) — 'the watchmen who circuit the city' — the same verb savav (to circuit) connects the beloved's search (v2: asobbevah) to the watchmen's circuit; she and they are doing the same action but for different purposes. The watchmen who encounter her do not arrest her (as in 5:7 where a later encounter is more violent); they are simply asked if they have seen the beloved. The question is not embarrassing; her public search for the one she loves is presented as urgent and appropriate.
4: כִּמְעַט שֶׁעָבַרְתִּי מֵהֶם (kim'at she'avarti mehem) — 'barely had I passed them' — the temporal clause expresses the speed of the finding; she had barely passed the watchmen when she found him. אָחַזְתִּיו (achaztihu) — 'I held him' — the Qal perfect with a possessive suffix; the holding is decisive, not tentative. לֹא אַרְפֶּנּוּ (lo arpennu) — 'I would not let him go' — a negative jussive expressing determination. The imagery of finding, holding, and bringing into the mother's house parallels Jacob's bringing Rebekah into his mother Sarah's tent after their first meeting (Genesis 24:67).
5: The refrain from 2:7 appears verbatim: I adjure you, daughters of Jerusalem, do not rouse love until it pleases. The reappearance of the adjuration after the finding (v4) — rather than after longing — suggests that love's timing is still the central concern even when love has been found: the mystery of love's movement cannot be controlled or forced even by those who have found it. The protective adjuration frames the entire dream-sequence.
6: מִי זֹאת עֹלָה מִן הַמִּדְבָּר (mi zot olah min hamidbar) — 'who is this coming up from the wilderness?' — the olah (coming up, ascending) is the same verb as the burnt offering (olah) and the pilgrimage ascent to Jerusalem (aliyah). The wilderness-to-city ascent is the Exodus pattern; this procession is fragrant with myrrh and frankincense and all the merchant's powders — the full array of the incense trade. כְּתִימְרוֹת עָשָׁן (ke-timrot ashan) — 'like columns of smoke' — the incense-smoke of worship ascending; the column of cloud in the Exodus narrative.
7: מִטָּתוֹ שֶׁלִּשְׁלֹמֹה (mitato shelishlomoh) — 'the litter/bed of Solomon' — mittah can mean a bed or a portable litter (a carrying bed for royalty). The identification of Solomon introduces the royal dimension that recurs in Song of Solomon 3-4. Sixty warriors surround the litter — the number sixty appears in military contexts throughout the ancient Near East (Ugaritic texts describe sixty heroes; the Psalms use sixty as a military round number).
8: אֲחֻזֵי חֶרֶב (achuzei cherev) — 'grasping/holding a sword' — the warriors are not merely armed but actively gripping their weapons, at constant readiness. מְלֻמְּדֵי מִלְחָמָה (melummedei milchamah) — 'trained in battle' — the Pual participle indicates formal training, professional soldiers rather than militia. מִפַּחַד בַּלֵּילוֹת (mippachad baleilot) — 'for fear in the nights' — the same plural 'nights' as v1; the night that is the realm of the beloved's searching is also the realm of danger requiring armed guard.
9: אַפִּרְיוֹן (apiryon) — 'royal litter/palanquin' — a hapax legomenon (used only here in the Hebrew Bible). Its etymology is disputed: some derive it from Sanskrit paryanka (couch) via Persian; others from Egyptian; still others from Greek phoreion (a portable chair). The foreign loanword emphasizes the exotic luxury of Solomon's royal vehicle. מֵעֲצֵי הַלְּבָנוֹן (me'atzei hallevanon) — 'from Lebanese cedar' — the premium building material, as in the Temple (1 Kings 5:6-10). The palanquin is built to temple standards.
10: עַמּוּדָיו עָשָׂה כֶסֶף (ammudav asah kesef) — 'its posts he made of silver' — the progressive enumeration silver (posts), gold (back/canopy), purple (seat), matches the order of materials used in the Tabernacle (Exod 26-27: the progression from outer court to the Most Holy Place uses silver, gold, and purple in ascending order of holiness). The interior of the palanquin is lined (murchav ahavah) — the MKT has 'lovingly lined by the daughters of Jerusalem' — but the Hebrew is difficult; rachav (paved/laid) with love, or love as the material.
11: צְאֶינָה (tze'enah) — 'come out, go forth' — feminine plural imperative addressed to 'daughters of Zion' — the first appearance of Tzion (Zion) rather than Yerushalayim (Jerusalem) in the Song. The shift to Zion carries the full weight of the covenant city. עֲטֶרֶת (atarah) — crown — the same word used for the crown of rejoicing (1 Thessalonians 2:19) and the crown placed on Job (Job 31:36). The crown given by the mother (immo) at the wedding day signals that the mother's role in the wedding investiture is recognized, unusual in ancient Near Eastern contexts where the father typically crowns the king.
